steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank must logically complete the researchers' hypothesis. The key evidence is: (1) most sediment is fine-grained mudstone deposited in low-flow water, (2) the fine grains show episodic desiccation cracks, and (3) coarse-grained sandstone appears in intervals, indicating periodic flowing water. Any correct answer must account for all three data points.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — a lake existed at the Murray Formation for a prolonged period, though the lake occasionally experienced drying and there were periods in which one or more streams were present.: The dominant fine-grained mudstone supports a prolonged lake; the desiccation cracks support occasional drying; the coarse sandstone intervals support periodic streams. All three evidence points are matched. ✓
  • Choice B — one or more streams existed at the Murray Formation for an extended period until being replaced by a lake that persisted for only a brief period before permanently drying.: This reverses the sequence (streams first, brief lake after) and says the lake dried permanently, contradicting the episodic rather than permanent desiccation implied by the cracks. ✗
  • Choice C — a stream fed lake was present at the Murray Formation for an extended period, and although the streams experienced occasional drying, the lake did not.: The desiccation cracks are in the fine-grained mudstone, which represents the lake environment, not the streams. Saying the lake never dried directly contradicts those cracks. ✗
  • Choice D — although the area of the Murray Formation experienced a prolonged period of dryness that prevented a lake from forming, water flowing from a distant source was present.: A prolonged period without a lake cannot explain why the dominant sediment is fine-grained mudstone, which requires sustained low-flow standing water. ✗
That is the correct answer.
